title: Type B — rectangular (resolution)
prompt:
A digital instrument has a **reading resolution** $r = 1\ \mu\text{m}$. The reading can lie anywhere inside one resolution step, so the error is modelled as a **rectangular (uniform)** distribution of half-width $a = r/2$.

Work in **base units (metres)**. Compute the half-width $a$ in **C5**, the standard uncertainty $u = a/\sqrt{3}$ in **C7**, and the variance $u^2(x)=a^2/3$ in **C9**.

checks.label: Half-width a
checks.teach: For a resolution $r$ the error band is $\pm r/2$, so the **half-width** is $a=r/2=0.5\ \mu\text{m}=5\times10^{-7}\,\text{m}$.

checks.label: Standard uncertainty u
checks.teach: A rectangular (uniform) distribution of half-width $a$ has standard deviation $a/\sqrt{3}$. This is the divisor $k_d=\sqrt{3}$ for **rectangular** Type B inputs.

checks.label: Variance u²(x)
checks.teach: The variance is $u^2=a^2/3$ — the form $a_j^2/k_d$ with $k_d=3$ that fills column $u^2(x_j)$ of the budget.

method: Resolution $r=1\,\mu$m → half-width $a=r/2=0.5\,\mu$m. Rectangular divisor: $u=a/\sqrt{3}=2.887\times10^{-7}$ m, variance $a^2/3=8.33\times10^{-14}\,\text{m}^2$.

title: Type B — triangular distribution
prompt:
When two rounded readings are combined (or the centre of the interval is more likely than the edges), the error is modelled as a **triangular** distribution of half-width $a$.

Here $a = 0.5\ \mu\text{m} = 5\times10^{-7}\ \text{m}$. Compute the standard uncertainty $u = a/\sqrt{6}$ in **C5** and the variance $u^2 = a^2/6$ in **C7**.

checks.label: Standard uncertainty u
checks.teach: A symmetric **triangular** distribution of half-width $a$ has standard deviation $a/\sqrt{6}$. Because the values pile up near the centre, it is **smaller** than the rectangular $a/\sqrt{3}$ for the same half-width.

checks.label: Variance u²(x)
checks.teach: Variance $u^2=a^2/6$. Divisor table for Type B inputs: rectangular $k_d=\sqrt{3}$, triangular $k_d=\sqrt{6}$, normal $k_d=k$ (the coverage factor).

title: Type B — normal with stated coverage
prompt:
A calibration certificate states the **bias** of an instrument as an **expanded uncertainty** $U = 3.7\ \mu	ext{m}$, given on the **average of $n_d = 4$** measurements at a **confidence level $P = 0.95$** (a normal/Student input).

The coverage factor is the Student-$t$ value for $
u = n_d-1$ d.o.f.: $k = $ **T.INV$(1-(1-P)/2,\ 
u)$**. Compute $
u$ in **C7**, $k$ in **C9**, and the standard uncertainty $s = U/k$ in **C11** (in metres; $U = 3.7	imes10^{-6}$ m).
hint: Divide the expanded uncertainty by its coverage factor: $s=U/k$.

given.A1: Type B — normal input given as expanded uncertainty

checks.label: Degrees of freedom ν
checks.teach: A bias declared on the average of $n_d$ measurements carries $\nu=n_d-1=3$ degrees of freedom — the same $\nu$ that later feeds Welch–Satterthwaite.

checks.label: Coverage factor k
checks.teach: The certificate's $U$ was obtained with a two-sided Student-$t$ factor. Recover it with $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}=$ T.INV$(1-(1-0.95)/2,\ 3)=3.182$. Use the **two-sided** quantile $1-(1-P)/2$.

checks.label: Standard uncertainty s
checks.teach: To turn an expanded uncertainty back into a **standard** uncertainty, divide by its coverage factor: $s=U/k=3.7\,\mu\text{m}/3.182=1.163\,\mu\text{m}$. This is column $s_j$ in the budget.

title: Type A — from a sample of repeated readings
prompt:
A **Type A** contribution is estimated statistically from repeated readings. Five repeated measurements (in metres) are listed in **B2:B6**. The reported result is their **average**, so the standard uncertainty of the mean is $u=s/\sqrt{n}$.

Compute the sample standard deviation $s$ (**STDEV.S**) in **C9**, the count $n$ in **C10**, and the standard uncertainty of the mean $u=s/\sqrt{n}$ in **C11**.
hint: Type A uses the data themselves: $s$ from STDEV.S, then $u=s/\sqrt n$ for an average.

given.A1: Type A — standard uncertainty of the mean
given.A11: Std. unc. of the mean  u = s/√n (m) =

checks.label: Sample std. dev. s
checks.teach: Type A uncertainty comes from statistics on the data: the sample standard deviation $s$ uses the $n-1$ divisor (STDEV.S), here $\approx 2.74\,\mu\text{m}$.

checks.label: Count n
checks.teach: There are $n=5$ readings; the degrees of freedom of this Type A estimate are $n-1=4$.

checks.label: Std. unc. of the mean
checks.teach: Because the **average** is reported, its standard uncertainty is $s/\sqrt{n}$ — the standard deviation of the mean. If a single reading (not the average) were reported, you would keep $s$ itself.

title: Repeatability & reproducibility → u(x)
prompt:
Two contributions of the canonical length measurement:

- **Repeatability** is declared by the maker as a **standard deviation** $\sigma=1.3\ \mu\text{m}$ (already a standard uncertainty).
- **Reproducibility** was tested over $10$ replications giving a **variance** $V=10.7\ \mu\text{m}^2$; the result averages **$n_r=5$** measurements, so its variance is $V/n_r$.

Work in metres. Put the repeatability $\sigma$ (m) in **C5**, the reproducibility standard deviation $\sqrt{V}$ (m) in **C7**, and its **variance of the mean** $u^2 = (\sqrt V)^2/n_r$ in **C9**.

given.A9: Reproducibility variance of mean  u² = V/n_r (m²) =

checks.label: Repeatability σ (m)
checks.teach: A **standard deviation** quoted by the maker is *already* a standard uncertainty: no divisor. Just convert µm → m: $1.3\,\mu\text{m}=1.3\times10^{-6}$ m.

checks.label: Reproducibility √V (m)
checks.teach: Reproducibility was given as a **variance** $V=10.7\,\mu\text{m}^2$, so its standard deviation is $\sqrt{V}=3.271\,\mu\text{m}$ (matches UncTab $H40$).

checks.label: Variance of the mean u²
checks.teach: Because the result is the **average of $n_r=5$**, the reproducibility variance is divided by $n_r$: $u^2=V/n_r=2.14\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. This is the $n_r$ factor in column $u^2(x_j)$ ($N40$).

method: Repeatability $\sigma=1.3\,\mu$m (no divisor). Reproducibility $\sqrt{10.7}=3.271\,\mu$m; averaged over $n_r=5$ → variance $10.7\times10^{-12}/5=2.14\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$.

title: Sensitivity coefficient cj = ∂y/∂xj
prompt:
A force is computed from $F = k\,x$ (a spring of stiffness $k$ stretched by $x$). The sensitivity coefficients are the partial derivatives.

Given $k = 410\ \text{N/m}$ and $x = 0.011\ \text{m}$, compute $F$ in **C7**, then $c_x = \partial F/\partial x = k$ in **C9** and $c_k = \partial F/\partial k = x$ in **C11**. (For a product, each variable's sensitivity is simply the *other* factor.)

checks.label: Force F
checks.teach: Evaluate the model at the nominal inputs: $F=kx=410\times0.011=4.51$ N.

checks.label: c_x = ∂F/∂x
checks.teach: $\partial(kx)/\partial x = k = 410\ \text{N/m}$. The sensitivity says how a unit change in $x$ moves $F$; its **units** carry through to $u_j(y)=c_j\,u(x_j)$.

checks.label: c_k = ∂F/∂k
checks.teach: $\partial(kx)/\partial k = x = 0.011\ \text{m}$. For a product $y=\prod x_i$ each factor's sensitivity is the product of the *others* — equivalently $c_j=y/x_j$.

method: $F=kx=4.51$ N. $c_x=k=410$, $c_k=x=0.011$. (Matches the Final-exam force sheet $F=4.51$ N, $c_k=0.011$, $c_x=410$.)

title: One budget row — contribution uj²(y)
prompt:
Combine an input's variance with its sensitivity to get its **contribution** to the output variance: $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)$.

Take the $\alpha$ (workpiece temperature-coefficient) row of the canonical budget: half-width $a_j = 2\times10^{-6}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, rectangular ($k_d=3$), sensitivity $c_j = -1.6001480$. Compute $u^2(x_j)=a_j^2/3$ in **C7**, then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)$ in **C9**.

checks.label: Variance u²(xj)
checks.teach: Rectangular input: $u^2(x_j)=a_j^2/3=1.333\times10^{-12}$. (UncTab $N41$.)

checks.label: Contribution uj²(y)
checks.teach: Propagate through the model: $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)=(-1.60015)^2\times1.333\times10^{-12}=3.414\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. Squaring $c_j$ removes its sign, so positive and negative sensitivities **both add** to the variance. (UncTab $P41$.)

title: Combine, Welch–Satterthwaite, expanded U
prompt:
The six contributions $u_j^2(y)$ of the canonical budget are pre-listed in **B3:B8** (m²) with their degrees of freedom $\nu_j$ in **C3:C8**. Finish the GUM budget at $P=0.95$.

1. Combined variance $u^2(y)=\sum u_j^2(y)$ in **E3**.
2. Combined standard uncertainty $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}$ in **E5**.
3. Effective d.o.f. $\nu_y=\left\lfloor \dfrac{u^4(y)}{\sum u_j^4(y)/\nu_j}\right\rfloor$ in **E7** (use **INT** for the floor).
4. Coverage factor $k=$ T.INV$(1-(1-P)/2,\ \nu_y)$ in **E9**.
5. Expanded uncertainty $U=k\,u(y)$ in **E11**.

given.A1: Combine contributions → effective dof → expanded U

checks.label: Combined variance u²(y)
checks.teach: Variances **add**: $u^2(y)=\sum_j u_j^2(y)=1.3012\times10^{-11}\,\text{m}^2$. (UncTab $P45$.)

checks.label: Combined std. unc. u(y)
checks.teach: Take the square root once, at the end: $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}=3.607\,\mu\text{m}$. (UncTab $P46$.)

checks.label: Effective d.o.f. ν_y
checks.teach:
**Welch–Satterthwaite**: $
u_y=u^4(y)/\sum(u_j^4(y)/
u_j)$. The raw value is $\approx 15.7$; you must take the **floor** (INT) → $
u_y=15$. Rounding up would overstate the d.o.f. and understate $k$. (UncTab $P47$.)

checks.label: Coverage factor k
checks.teach: With $\nu_y=15$ finite d.o.f. the factor is the Student-$t$ quantile, **not** 1.96: $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.1314$. (UncTab $P49$.)

checks.label: Expanded uncertainty U(y)
checks.teach: Finally $U=k\,u(y)=2.1314\times3.607\,\mu\text{m}=7.69\,\mu\text{m}$. Report the result as $y\pm U$ at $P=95\%$. (UncTab $P50$.)

method: Sum → $u^2(y)=1.3012\times10^{-11}$; $u(y)=3.607\,\mu$m; W–S floor → $\nu_y=15$; $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.1314$; $U=7.69\,\mu$m. Exactly the canonical *Uncertainty Table* output ($P45$–$P50$).

title: Capstone — full uncertainty budget (Uncertainty Table)
prompt:
The full **GUM uncertainty budget** of the canonical *Uncertainty Table* exam example. A length $L_T=800.074$ mm is measured; the model corrects it to $20^\circ$C, $L_{20}=L_T[1+(\alpha-\beta)(20-T)]$. The six contributions are laid out as a **table, one row per contribution**.

**Columns:** B = $u(x_j)$ value (a std-dev $s_j$, or a half-width $a_j$); C = divisor (1 = already standard; 3 = rectangular; 2 = temperature interval); D = $n_d$; E = $n_r$; F = sensitivity $c_j$. **You fill G = $u^2(x_j)=\dfrac{B^2}{C}\cdot\dfrac{D}{E}$ and H = $u_j^2(y)=F^2\,G$.** Then combine in H11–H16. Everything in **base units (m, °C)**; $P=0.95$.

**Traps:** (1) bias, repeatability and reproducibility are already *standard* (divisor 1); resolution & temp-coefficients are rectangular (divisor 3). (2) bias folds in $n_d=4$ (×4), resolution & reproducibility average over $n_r=5$ (÷5). (3) take the **floor** of the Welch–Satterthwaite dof. (4) $k$ is Student-$t$, not 1.96.

given.A1: Full GUM uncertainty budget — length corrected to 20 °C

checks.label: u²(x) bias
checks.teach: Bias is *standard* (divisor 1) but is folded over $n_d=4$ measurements: $u^2=s^2\cdot4=5.407\times10^{-12}$. (UncTab $N37$.)

checks.label: u²(x) resolution
checks.teach: Resolution is **rectangular** ($\div3$) and averaged over $n_r=5$: $u^2=(a^2/3)/5=1.667\times10^{-14}$. (UncTab $N39$.)

checks.label: u²(x) reproducibility
checks.teach: Reproducibility std-dev $3.271\,\mu$m, divisor 1, averaged over $n_r=5$: $u^2=10.7\times10^{-12}/5=2.14\times10^{-12}$. (UncTab $N40$.)

checks.label: u²(x) α
checks.teach: Workpiece coeff. $\alpha$, rectangular: $u^2=(2\times10^{-6})^2/3=1.333\times10^{-12}\,°\text{C}^{-2}$. (UncTab $N41$.)

checks.label: u²(x) β
checks.teach: Instrument coeff. $\beta$, rectangular: $u^2=(2\times10^{-7})^2/3=1.333\times10^{-14}$. (UncTab $N42$.)

checks.label: u²(x) T
checks.teach: Temperature interval $\pm5\,°$C with divisor 2 ($k_a$): $u^2=25/2=12.5\,°\text{C}^2$. (UncTab $N43$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) bias
checks.teach: $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. With $c\approx1$ the bias passes through: $5.407\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. (UncTab $P37$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) reproducibility
checks.teach: $c\approx1$: $2.14\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. Together with the bias these dominate the budget. (UncTab $P40$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) α
checks.teach: $c_\alpha=-1.6001$: $u_j^2(y)=(-1.6001)^2\times1.333\times10^{-12}=3.414\times10^{-12}$. The sign of $c$ is lost on squaring. (UncTab $P41$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) β
checks.teach: $c_\beta=+1.6001$: $3.414\times10^{-14}$ — small, since $\beta$'s half-width is 10× smaller than $\alpha$'s. (UncTab $P42$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) T
checks.teach: $c_T=4.0004\times10^{-7}$ (the corrected model is only weakly $T$-sensitive near $20\,°$C): $u_j^2(y)=2.000\times10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. (UncTab $P43$.)

checks.label: Combined variance u²(y)
checks.teach: Sum every row's $u_j^2(y)$: $u^2(y)=1.3012\times10^{-11}\,\text{m}^2$. (UncTab $P45$.)

checks.label: Combined u(y)
checks.teach: $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}=3.607\,\mu\text{m}$. (UncTab $P46$.)

checks.label: Effective dof ν_y
checks.teach:
Welch–Satterthwaite with each row's $
u_j$ (bias 3, resolution 100, reprod. 9, $\alpha$ 30, $ eta$ 30, $T$ 30): $
u_y=\lfloor u^4(y)/\sum(u_j^4(y)/
u_j)floor=15$. The **floor** is essential. (UncTab $P47$.)

checks.label: Coverage factor k
checks.teach: $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.1314$ — Student-$t$, larger than the asymptotic 1.96 because $\nu_y$ is finite. (UncTab $P49$.)

checks.label: Expanded uncertainty U(y)
checks.teach: $U=k\,u(y)=2.1314\times3.607\,\mu\text{m}=7.69\,\mu\text{m}$. Report $L_{20}=800.0748\ \text{mm}\pm7.7\,\mu\text{m}$ at 95%. This reproduces the exam's *Uncertainty Table* exactly. (UncTab $P50$.)

method: Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\cdot\frac{n_d}{n_r}$ then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. Bias ($5.41\times10^{-12}$) and reproducibility ($2.14\times10^{-12}$) dominate. $u^2(y)=1.3012\times10^{-11}$, $u(y)=3.607\,\mu$m, W–S floor $\nu_y=15$, $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.131$, $U=7.69\,\mu$m. Reproduces *Uncertainty Table - example* (Solution sheet $N37$–$P50$).

title: Worked example — strain rosette ε₁ (Uncertainty examples 1·02)
prompt:
From *Uncertainty examples 1*, exercise 02. A strain-gauge rosette gives the principal strain $\varepsilon_1$. We build the **bias** contribution of gauge $a$. **Bias is given as a relative standard deviation** $0.001$ (0.1%) of the strain, on the mean of **$n_d=5$** measurements ($n_r=1$).

Strain $\varepsilon_a=1440$ mm/m → multiply by $10^{-6}$ for base units. Sensitivity $c_a=1.1603710$.

In base units, compute $s_a=0.001\,(\varepsilon_a\cdot10^{-6})$ in **C7**; the variance $u^2(x_a)=s_a^2\cdot n_d$ (with $n_d=5$) in **C9**; and the contribution $u_a^2(y)=c_a^2\,u^2(x_a)$ in **C11**.
hint: Relative bias: $s=0.001\cdot\varepsilon$. Sheet uses $u^2(x)=s^2\cdot n_d/n_r$ with $n_d=5,n_r=1$.

given.A1: Strain rosette ε₁ — bias contribution of gauge a

checks.label: Bias std. dev. s_a
checks.teach: A **relative** standard deviation multiplies the measured value: $s_a=0.001\times(1440\times10^{-6})=1.44\times10^{-6}$. Watch the base-unit conversion mm/m → pure strain ($\times10^{-6}$). (Ex.1·02 $H36$.)

checks.label: Variance u²(x_a)
checks.teach: The sheet's column is $u^2(x_j)=s_j^2\,n_d/n_r$. With $n_d=5,\,n_r=1$: $u^2(x_a)=s_a^2\times5=1.0368\times10^{-11}$. (Ex.1·02 $N36$.)

method: $s_a=0.001\cdot\varepsilon_a=1.44\times10^{-6}$; $u^2(x_a)=s_a^2\cdot5=1.0368\times10^{-11}$; $u_a^2(y)=c_a^2 u^2(x_a)=1.396\times10^{-11}$. Exactly the cached *Uncertainty examples 1*, sheet 02 ($H36,N36,P36$).

title: Worked example — thermometer waiting time (Uncertainty examples 2·01)
prompt:
From *Uncertainty examples 2*, exercise 01. The waiting time is $t=-\dfrac{C_m}{k_t}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_m}{\Delta T}\right)$ with $C_m=0.1$, $k_t=15\,$mW/°C $=0.015$, $S_m=0.1$, $\Delta T=25$.

Compute $t$ in **C9**. Then build the **bias of $S_m$** contribution: expanded $U_a=0.05$ at $P=0.95$ on $\nu=7$ d.o.f. gives $k=$T.INV$(0.975,7)$ in **C11**, standard $s=U_a/k$ in **C13**, and variance $u^2(x)=s^2\cdot n_d$ with $n_d=4$ in **C15**.

given.A1: Thermometer waiting time t and bias of Sm
given.A11: Coverage of the bias  k = T.INV(0.975, 7) =

checks.label: Coverage k of bias
checks.teach: The bias $U_a$ was an expanded uncertainty at 95% with $\nu=7$: $k=$T.INV$(0.975,7)=2.3646$. (Ex.2·01 $G33$, two-sided form.)

checks.label: Variance u²(x_Sm)
checks.teach: Variance with $n_d=4$ averaged measurements: $u^2(x)=s^2\cdot n_d=0.02115^2\times4=1.788\times10^{-3}$. (Ex.2·01 $N33$.)

title: Full budget — thread diameter d₂ by three wires (Uncertainty examples 1·01)
prompt:
From *Uncertainty examples 1*, exercise 01 — the full GUM budget. The average diameter of an external thread is found from a micrometer length $M$ over three calibrated wires of diameter $d$, pitch $p$ and flank angle $a$: $d_2 = M - d\,(1+1/\sin(a/2)) + p/(2\tan(a/2))$.

Six contributions feed the budget (work in **base units**, m and rad). Each row gives **B** = $u(x_j)$ value (a half-width $a_j$ or a standard deviation $s_j$), **C** = divisor $k_d$ (1 = already standard, 3 = rectangular, 2 = interval), **D** = $n_d$ count folded in, **E** = $n_r$ averaged, **F** = sensitivity $c_j$. **Fill G** $=u^2(x_j)=\dfrac{B^2}{C}\cdot\dfrac{D}{E}$ **and H** $=u_j^2(y)=F^2\,G$, then combine in H9–H14. Confidence $P=0.996$ (risk $\alpha_e=0.004$).

**Notes:** the micrometer **bias** comes from a *maximum spread* $E_p=4\,\mu$m (half-width $a=E_p/2=2\,\mu$m, rectangular); **reproducibility** is a standard uncertainty $R_{pr}=2.5\,\mu$m averaged over $N_m=4$; **resolution** $r_p=1\,\mu$m → $a=r_p/2$, rectangular, averaged over 4; $T_d=1\,\mu$m (rect, ÷3), $T_p=0.01$ mm is a half-range (÷2), $T_a=1^\circ$ → radians (rect, ÷3).
hint: Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\cdot\frac{D}{E}$, then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)$. Floor the Welch–Satterthwaite dof.

given.A1: Thread d₂ by three wires — full GUM budget (P = 0.996)

checks.label: u²(x) M bias
checks.teach: The micrometer **bias** is given as a *maximum spread* $E_p=4\,\mu$m, so the half-width is $a=E_p/2=2\,\mu$m and the distribution is **rectangular** ($\div3$): $u^2=(2\times10^{-6})^2/3=1.333\times10^{-12}$. (Ex.1·01 $N33$.)

checks.label: u²(x) reproducibility
checks.teach: Reproducibility $R_{pr}=2.5\,\mu$m is *already a standard uncertainty* (divisor 1) but the result averages $N_m=4$ measurements, so $u^2=s^2/4=(2.5\times10^{-6})^2/4=1.5625\times10^{-12}$. (Ex.1·01 $N34$.)

checks.label: u²(x) resolution
checks.teach: Resolution $r_p=1\,\mu$m → $a=0.5\,\mu$m, rectangular ($\div3$), averaged over 4: $u^2=(a^2/3)/4=2.083\times10^{-14}$. (Ex.1·01 $N35$.)

checks.label: u²(x) d
checks.teach: Wire diameter tolerance $T_d=1\,\mu$m, rectangular, with $n_r=3$ wires: $u^2=(1\times10^{-6})^2/3/3=1.111\times10^{-13}$. (Ex.1·01 $N36$.)

checks.label: u²(x) p
checks.teach: Pitch cyclic error $T_p=0.01$ mm $=10^{-5}$ m is a **half-range**, so divisor 2: $u^2=(10^{-5})^2/2=5\times10^{-11}$. (Ex.1·01 $N37$.)

checks.label: u²(x) a
checks.teach: Flank-angle tolerance $T_a=1^\circ=0.017453$ rad, rectangular: $u^2=(0.017453)^2/3=1.015\times10^{-4}\,\text{rad}^2$. Convert degrees to radians first. (Ex.1·01 $N38$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) M bias
checks.teach: $c_M\approx1$ (the model is linear in $M$): the bias passes straight through, $1.333\times10^{-12}$. (Ex.1·01 $P33$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) reproducibility
checks.teach: $c_M\approx1$: $1.5625\times10^{-12}$. Bias and reproducibility of $M$ together are large but not dominant here. (Ex.1·01 $P34$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) d
checks.teach: $c_d=-3.0$: with three wires a change in $d$ moves $d_2$ three-fold, so $u_j^2(y)=(-3)^2\times1.111\times10^{-13}=1.0\times10^{-12}$. The sign vanishes on squaring. (Ex.1·01 $P36$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) p
checks.teach: $c_p=\sin60^\circ=0.8660$: $u_j^2(y)=0.866^2\times5\times10^{-11}=3.75\times10^{-11}$. This is the **dominant** contribution — the pitch tolerance is where to spend effort. (Ex.1·01 $P37$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) a
checks.teach: $c_a=-4.26\times10^{-4}$ is tiny, but $u^2(x_a)$ is huge, so $u_j^2(y)=1.84\times10^{-11}$ — the second-largest term. (Ex.1·01 $P38$.)

checks.label: Effective dof ν_y
checks.teach:
Welch–Satterthwaite with each row's $
u_j$ (column I: M-bias 5, reprod. 9, res. 100, $d$ 30, $p$ 30, $a$ 30). The large $
u$ on the dominant $p$ and $a$ rows pushes $
u_y$ up to the **floor** value 60. (Ex.1·01 $P42$.)

checks.label: Coverage factor k
checks.teach: At the tight $P=0.996$ (risk $\alpha_e=0.004$) with $\nu_y=60$: $k=$T.INV$(1-0.004/2,\,60)=2.994$. A higher confidence demands a larger $k$. (Ex.1·01 $P44$.)

method: Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\cdot\frac{n_d}{n_r}$, then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. The pitch ($c_p=\sin60^\circ$, $3.75\times10^{-11}$) and the flank angle ($1.84\times10^{-11}$) dominate. $u^2(y)=5.985\times10^{-11}$, $u(y)=7.736\,\mu$m, W–S floor $\nu_y=60$, $k=$T.INV$(0.998,60)=2.994$, $U=23.2\,\mu$m. Reproduces *Uncertainty examples 1*, sheet 01 ($N33$–$P45$).

title: Full budget — metrological pressure gauge P (Uncertainty examples 2·02)
prompt:
From *Uncertainty examples 2*, exercise 02 — the full GUM budget. A pressure $P=\dfrac{m\,g\,(1-\rho_a/\rho_m)}{\pi\,((d+D)/4)^2}$ is produced by a mass $m$ on a piston–cylinder of diameters $d,D$, with air buoyancy ($\rho_a$) and mass density ($\rho_m$).

Nine contributions feed the budget (work in **base units**: kg, m, m/s², kg/m³, Pa). Each row gives **B** = $u(x_j)$, **C** = divisor $k_d$, **D** = $n_d$, **E** = $n_r$, **F** = sensitivity $c_j$ (column I holds $\nu_j$). **Fill G** $=\dfrac{B^2}{C}\cdot\dfrac{D}{E}$ **and H** $=F^2\,G$, then combine in H12–H17 at $P=0.96$ (risk 0.04).

**Certificate rows ($\rho_m$, $g$):** the bias is an *expanded* uncertainty, so first recover its standard form $s=U/k$ with $k=$T.INV. **Setup checks C19–C21** do this; the resulting $s$ is already entered in column B for those rows. The two **diameter reproducibility** rows ($d,D$, $u_j^2(y)\approx6.6\times10^{3}$ Pa² each) dominate the budget.
hint: Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\cdot\frac{D}{E}$, then $c_j^2$ times it. Certificate bias: $s=U/k$, $k$ from T.INV.

given.A1: Pressure gauge P = m·g·(1−ρa/ρm)/(π((d+D)/4)²) — full GUM budget (P = 0.96)
given.G18: νj in column I; D & D-bias rows reuse the d sensitivity

checks.label: ρm coverage k
checks.teach: The $\rho_m$ bias is an *expanded* uncertainty $U=0.8\,$g/dm³ at risk 0.02 (P=0.98) with a large $\nu=100$: $k=$T.INV$(0.99,100)=2.3642$. (Ex.2·02 $G61$.)

checks.label: ρm std. unc. s
checks.teach: Standard form $s=U/k=0.8/2.3642=0.3384\,$kg/m³ (work in base units kg/m³). This is the value pre-entered in B6. (Ex.2·02 $H61$.)

checks.label: g coverage k
checks.teach: The gravity bias is a *relative* expanded uncertainty $4\times10^{-6}$ at P=0.997 on only $\nu=8$ dof, so $k=$T.INV$(0.9985,8)=4.199$ — large, because both the confidence is high and the dof are few. Then $s=(4\times10^{-6}\cdot g)/k$ gives B7. (Ex.2·02 $G62$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m bias
checks.teach: Balance bias $\pm23\,$mg is a half-width, rectangular: $u^2=(23\times10^{-6})^2/3=1.763\times10^{-10}\,\text{kg}^2$. (Ex.2·02 $N58$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m resolution
checks.teach: Resolution 10 mg → $a=5\,$mg, rectangular: $u^2=(5\times10^{-6})^2/3=8.333\times10^{-12}$. (Ex.2·02 $N59$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m reproducibility
checks.teach: Reproducibility given as a *max difference* 28 mg → half-width $a=14\,$mg, rectangular, $\nu=n-1=19$: $u^2=(14\times10^{-6})^2/3=6.533\times10^{-11}$. (Ex.2·02 $N60$.)

checks.label: u²(x) ρm bias
checks.teach: $\rho_m$ standard uncertainty $s=0.3384$ (divisor 1): $u^2=s^2=0.1145\,(\text{kg/m}^3)^2$. (Ex.2·02 $N61$.)

checks.label: u²(x) g bias
checks.teach: $g$ standard uncertainty $s$ (divisor 1) folded over $n_d=4$ measurements: $u^2=s^2\cdot4=3.490\times10^{-10}\,(\text{m/s}^2)^2$. (Ex.2·02 $N62$.)

checks.label: u²(x) d bias
checks.teach: Piston-diameter max error $\pm0.08\,\mu$m → $a=8\times10^{-8}$ m, rectangular: $u^2=2.133\times10^{-15}\,\text{m}^2$. (Ex.2·02 $N63$.)

checks.label: u²(x) d resolution
checks.teach: Diameter resolution 0.01 µm → $a=5\times10^{-9}$ m, rectangular: $u^2=8.333\times10^{-18}$. (Ex.2·02 $N64$.)

checks.label: u²(x) d reproducibility
checks.teach: Diameter reproducibility from a *variance* $0.0128\,\mu$m² → $s=\sqrt{0.0128}\,\mu$m $=1.131\times10^{-7}$ m (divisor 1): $u^2=s^2=1.28\times10^{-14}$. (Ex.2·02 $N65$.)

checks.label: u²(x) D bias
checks.teach: The cylinder diameter $D$ has identical metrology to $d$, so its bias variance equals the $d$ bias: $2.133\times10^{-15}$. (Ex.2·02 $N66$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) ρm bias
checks.teach: $c_{\rho m}=0.0493$ Pa/(kg/m³) is tiny, so despite a large $u^2(x)$ the contribution is only $2.78\times10^{-4}\,$Pa² — the **smallest** term. (Ex.2·02 $P61$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) d bias
checks.teach: $c_d=-7.16\times10^{8}$ Pa/m is enormous (the area is in the denominator squared): $u_j^2(y)=1.094\times10^{3}\,$Pa². (Ex.2·02 $P63$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) d reproducibility
checks.teach: $u_j^2(y)=6.563\times10^{3}\,$Pa² — the **largest** single contribution. The diameter reproducibility is where to invest to cut $U$. (Ex.2·02 $P65$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) D bias
checks.teach: $D$ contributes identically to $d$: $1.094\times10^{3}\,$Pa². (Ex.2·02 $P66$.)

checks.label: Combined variance u²(y)
checks.teach: Sum the nine table rows plus the three not laid out here (the $D$ resolution $4.27$, $D$ reproducibility $6563.4$ — equal to $d$ — and the $\rho_a$ bias $2.98$): $u^2(y)=1.5424\times10^{4}\,$Pa². Note the **two diameter-reproducibility rows together (≈13 130 Pa²) make up most of it.** (Ex.2·02 $P71$.)

checks.label: Effective dof ν_y
checks.teach:
Welch–Satterthwaite over **all twelve** contributions $\sum u_j^4(y)/
u_j$ (the nine table rows plus the off-table $D$-resolution, $D$-reproducibility and $ho_a$ rows). The two diameter-reproducibility terms ($
u=9$) dominate the denominator; the floor gives $
u_y=23$. (Ex.2·02 $P73$.)

checks.label: Expanded uncertainty U(y)
checks.teach: $U=k\,u(y)=2.177\times124.2=270.4\,$Pa. With the target $U=140\,$Pa unmet, the sheet asks for more diameter replications. Report $P\pm U$ at 96%. (Ex.2·02 $P76$.)

method: Certificate biases first: $\rho_m$ $k=$T.INV$(0.99,100)=2.364$, $s=0.8/k=0.338$; $g$ $k=$T.INV$(0.9985,8)=4.199$, $s=(4\times10^{-6}g)/k=9.34\times10^{-6}$. Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\frac{n_d}{n_r}$, $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. The **diameter reproducibility** of $d$ and $D$ ($6.56\times10^{3}$ Pa² each) dominate. $u^2(y)=1.542\times10^{4}$, $u(y)=124.2\,$Pa, W–S floor $\nu_y=23$, $k=$T.INV$(0.98,23)=2.177$, $U=270.4\,$Pa. Reproduces *Uncertainty examples 2*, sheet 02 ($N58$–$P76$). (The full sheet also carries a $D$-resolution, $D$-reproducibility and $\rho_a$ row, folded into the H12 sum.)

title: Full budget — force from a calibrated mass & local gravity (Uncertainty examples 3·01)
prompt:
From *Summary Exercises · Uncertainty examples 3*. A known force is $F=m\,g_{loc}=m\,(g_{ass}+\Delta g+\gamma\,h)$: a calibrated mass $m$ in the local gravity, transferred from an absolute value $g_{ass}$ by a gravimeter difference $\Delta g$ and a height gradient $\gamma$ over height $h$.

Ten contributions feed the budget (work in **base units**: kg, m/s², m, N). Each row gives **B** = $u(x_j)$, **C** = divisor $k_d$, **D** = $n_d$, **E** = $n_r$, **F** = sensitivity $c_j$ ($\nu_j$ in column I). **Fill G** $=\dfrac{B^2}{C}\cdot\dfrac{D}{E}$ **and H** $=F^2\,G$, then combine in H15–H20 at $P=0.95$.

**Notes:** the mass **bias** is an *expanded* uncertainty (P=0.98, $\nu=6$) → setup checks C23–C24 give $k$ and $s=U/k$ (entered in B3); the mass repeatability & resolution average over $n=3$ placements; $\Delta g$ reproducibility comes from a *variance* ($\sqrt{V}$); the gradient $\gamma$ is a regression slope with $\nu=n-2=7$.
hint: Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\cdot\frac{D}{E}$, then $c_j^2$ times it. Mass bias: $s=U/k$, $k=$T.INV. Regression slope: $\nu=n-2$.

given.A1: Force F = m·(gass + Δg + γ·h) — full GUM budget (P = 0.95)

checks.label: mass-bias coverage k
checks.teach: The mass bias is an *expanded* uncertainty $U=1\,$mg at risk 0.02 (P=0.98) on $\nu=6$ dof: $k=$T.INV$(0.99,6)=3.143$. (Ex.3 $G50$.)

checks.label: mass-bias std. unc. s
checks.teach: Standard form $s=U/k=10^{-6}/3.143=3.182\times10^{-7}\,$kg (base units). This value is pre-entered in B3. (Ex.3 $H50$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m bias
checks.teach: Mass-bias standard uncertainty (divisor 1) folded over $n_d=7$ measurements: $u^2=s^2\cdot7=7.088\times10^{-13}\,\text{kg}^2$. (Ex.3 $N50$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m repeatability
checks.teach: Repeatability $0.5\,$mg is a standard deviation (divisor 1) averaged over $n_r=3$ placements: $u^2=(5\times10^{-7})^2/3=8.333\times10^{-14}$. (Ex.3 $N51$.)

checks.label: u²(x) m resolution
checks.teach: Resolution $0.1\,$mg → $a=5\times10^{-8}$ kg, rectangular, averaged over 3: $u^2=(a^2/3)/3=2.778\times10^{-16}$. (Ex.3 $N52$.)

checks.label: u²(x) gass bias
checks.teach: Absolute-gravity max error $\pm10\,\mu$m/s² → $a=10^{-5}$, rectangular: $u^2=(10^{-5})^2/3=3.333\times10^{-11}$. (Ex.3 $N53$.)

checks.label: u²(x) gass repeatability
checks.teach: Gravimeter repeatability $1.5\,\mu$m/s² is a standard deviation: $u^2=(1.5\times10^{-6})^2=2.25\times10^{-12}$. (Ex.3 $N54$.)

checks.label: u²(x) gass resolution
checks.teach: Resolution $1\,\mu$m/s² → $a=5\times10^{-7}$, rectangular: $u^2=8.333\times10^{-14}$. (Ex.3 $N55$.)

checks.label: u²(x) Δg reproducibility
checks.teach: $\Delta g$ reproducibility from a *variance* $0.01\,(\mu$m/s²)² → $s=\sqrt{0.01}\,\mu$m/s² $=10^{-7}$ m/s² (divisor 1), $\nu=n-1=4$: $u^2=s^2=10^{-14}$. (Ex.3 $N56$.)

checks.label: u²(x) γ
checks.teach: The gradient $\gamma$ is a **regression slope**: its standard uncertainty $10^{-8}$ is already $u$ (divisor 1) with $\nu=n-2=7$ degrees of freedom: $u^2=10^{-16}$. (Ex.3 $N58$.)

checks.label: u²(x) h
checks.teach: Height standard uncertainty $1\,$mm (divisor 1) on the mean of $n_d=4$: $u^2=(10^{-3})^2\cdot4=4\times10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$. (Ex.3 $N59$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) m bias
checks.teach: $c_m=g_{loc}=9.805$ N/kg: $u_j^2(y)=9.805^2\times7.088\times10^{-13}=6.814\times10^{-11}\,\text{N}^2$ — the **dominant** contribution. (Ex.3 $P50$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) m repeatability
checks.teach: $8.011\times10^{-12}\,\text{N}^2$ — second-largest. (Ex.3 $P51$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) gass bias
checks.teach: $c_{gass}=m=1.5$ kg: $u_j^2(y)=1.5^2\times3.333\times10^{-11}=7.500\times10^{-11}\,\text{N}^2$ — also dominant (the absolute-gravity bias). (Ex.3 $P53$.)

checks.label: uj²(y) h
checks.teach: $c_h=m\,\gamma=4.13\times10^{-7}$ is minuscule (the gradient term barely changes $g_{loc}$): $6.811\times10^{-19}\,\text{N}^2$ — the smallest term. (Ex.3 $P59$.)

checks.label: Combined variance u²(y)
checks.teach: Sum the ten rows: $u^2(y)=1.5664\times10^{-10}\,\text{N}^2$. The mass bias, mass repeatability and absolute-gravity bias dominate. (Ex.3 $P61$.)

checks.label: Effective dof ν_y
checks.teach:
Welch–Satterthwaite: the mass-bias ($
u=6$) and absolute-gravity-bias ($
u=5$) terms, being both large and low-dof, pull $
u_y$ down; the floor gives $
u_y=12$. (Ex.3 $P63$.)

checks.label: Coverage factor k
checks.teach: At $P=0.95$ with $\nu_y=12$: $k=$T.INV$(0.975,12)=2.179$ — noticeably above 1.96 because the dof are few. (Ex.3 $P65$.)

method: Mass bias first: $k=$T.INV$(0.99,6)=3.143$, $s=10^{-6}/k=3.182\times10^{-7}$. Per row $u^2(x_j)=\frac{B^2}{C}\frac{n_d}{n_r}$, $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ with $c_m=g_{loc}=9.805$, $c_{gass}=c_{\Delta g}=m=1.5$, $c_\gamma=mh$, $c_h=m\gamma$. The mass bias ($6.8\times10^{-11}$), absolute-gravity bias ($7.5\times10^{-11}$) and mass repeatability ($8.0\times10^{-12}$) dominate. $u^2(y)=1.566\times10^{-10}$, $u(y)=12.5\,\mu$N, W–S floor $\nu_y=12$, $k=$T.INV$(0.975,12)=2.179$, $U=27.3\,\mu$N. Reproduces *Uncertainty examples 3*, sheet 01 ($N50$–$P66$).
